---
title: "Summary - NFL Home Field Advantage"
format: pdf
editor: visual
---

## Overview

The goal of this analysis was to investigate whether home field advantage is a real phenomenon in the NFL during the Mahomes era (2018 - 2023). To do this we looked at home win rate by season and tested whether it was meaningfully different from 50%, where 50% would suggest no advantage at all.

Super Bowl games and tied games were removed from the dataset before analysis.

## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)

## load and clean the raw data
nfl_raw <- read_csv("nfl_mahomes_era_games.csv")

nfl_filtered <- nfl_raw %>%
  filter(game_type != "SB") %>%   #remove super bowls
  filter(score_diff != 0)         #remove tie games

## reshape to one row per team per game
nfl_vars <- nfl_filtered %>%
  mutate(total_points = home_score + away_score)

home_teams <- nfl_vars %>%
  mutate(
    team           = home_team,
    opponent       = away_team,
    points_for     = home_score,
    points_against = away_score,
    location       = "Home",
    result         = ifelse(game_outcome == 1, "Win", "Loss")
  )

away_teams <- nfl_vars %>%
  mutate(
    team           = away_team,
    opponent       = home_team,
    points_for     = away_score,
    points_against = home_score,
    location       = "Away",
    result         = ifelse(game_outcome == 0, "Win", "Loss")
  )

nfl_clean <- bind_rows(home_teams, away_teams) %>%
  arrange(team, season, week) %>%
  select(season, game_type, week, team, opponent, location,
         points_for, points_against, result, total_points, game_id) %>%
  mutate(
    season   = as.factor(season),
    result   = as.factor(result),
    location = as.factor(location)
  )

## summarise home win rate by season
season_summary <- nfl_clean %>%
  filter(location == "Home") %>%
  group_by(season) %>%
  summarise(
    games    = n(),
    win_rate = mean(result == "Win"),
    .groups  = "drop"
  )

## calculate p-value
home_games <- sum(season_summary$games)
home_wins  <- round(sum(season_summary$win_rate * season_summary$games))

prop_test  <- prop.test(x = home_wins, n = home_games, p = 0.5)

## plot
season_summary %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = season, y = win_rate, group = 1)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "black") +
  geom_line(color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "red") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(win_rate, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(),
                     limits = c(0.4, 0.73)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Home win rate by season",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = 50% (no advantage)",
    x        = "Season",
    y        = "Home win rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

## Interpretation

The plot shows that home teams won more than 50% of their games in every season except 2020, where the win rate dropped to 49.8% - essentially a coin flip. This is unlikely to be a coincidence. The 2020 season was played largely without fans due to COVID-19 restrictions, which strongly suggests that crowd noise and atmosphere play a meaningful role in the home advantage.

After fans returned in 2021 the win rate climbed back above 50% and continued to rise, reaching 56.9% in 2022 before settling at 56.3% in 2023. The overall p-value supports the conclusion that the home advantage across this period is statistically significant and not due to chance.

The 2018 season stands out with a notably high win rate of 59.8%, which may reflect natural variation in a single season rather than a structural shift. Overall the data does suggest that home field advantage is a real phenomenon in the NFL, and the 2020 dip points to crowd noise as one of the more likely drivers of that effect.
